summary.title: Public governance contract
summary.independence_note: This repository runs on its own public Python, static-export, and browser stack. It is not a thin shell over a private LOOPCHii platform service.
summary.claim_boundary: Governance here means public evidence, visible controls, and explicit human-review thresholds. It does not mean private-platform enforcement has been open-sourced.

domains.label: Algorithmic fairness
domains.headline: The fairness lane is usable, but it is not self-certifying.
domains.note: Industry-level readiness is B-. Diversity (0.713), parity (0.744), dialogue equity (0.743), and screen equity (0.761) all need to travel together before this repo should present a calm fairness story.

evidence.label: Industry grade
evidence.note: Compressed read across the four representation dimensions.

evidence.label: Parity point
evidence.note: Centered closer to 1.0 means less headline imbalance.

evidence.label: Diversity point
evidence.note: Breadth matters, but it is not the whole fairness picture.

evidence.label: Average confidence spread
evidence.note: Wider bands mean the fairness claim should stay softer.

domains.label: Discrimination pressure
domains.headline: The effect sizes are too large to wave away as presentation noise.
domains.note: Dialogue-related group differences stay material. The strongest current effect is gender dialogue at Cramér's V 0.576 (large). That means this surface should raise structural-risk questions, not only summarize distribution.

evidence.label: Mean effect pressure
evidence.note: Average Cramér's V across the measured dialogue disparity tests.

evidence.label: Gender dialogue
evidence.note: If this stays high, role voice is still stratified.

evidence.label: Racial dialogue
evidence.note: High values mean speaking share is not distributing neutrally.

evidence.label: Age dialogue
evidence.note: Generational visibility can flatten long before it is obvious in the UI.

domains.label: Data-scope discipline
domains.headline: The public scope is tight enough to inspect without pretending to be a private trust platform.
domains.note: The repo keeps synthetic representation data and public music rows in separate lanes, exposes the contract for both, and avoids direct contact or location fields in the current contracted schemas. That is strong public-scope discipline.

evidence.label: Contracted datasets
evidence.note: Separate lanes help keep methods and claims honest.

evidence.label: Risky fields detected
evidence.note: Count of direct-identifier style field names in the public schemas.

evidence.label: Lane separation
evidence.note: Synthetic and public lanes should remain visibly distinct.

evidence.label: Manual refresh disclosure
evidence.note: The music lane does not pretend to be a hidden live ingestion firehose.

domains.label: GDPR and consent boundary
domains.headline: Useful for public research, not sufficient for subject-rights automation.
domains.note: This repository is an open-source analysis repo. It does not implement lawful-basis routing, DSAR handling, deletion receipts, or per-subject consent tracking. That is the right boundary for open work, and it should stay visible.

evidence.label: Personal contact fields
evidence.note: The current public schemas avoid obvious personal-contact columns.

evidence.label: Subject-rights flows
evidence.note: There is no DSAR or deletion orchestration in this repo today.

evidence.label: Refresh posture
evidence.note: Public-source refreshes are explicit instead of silently continuous.

evidence.label: Public-source lane
evidence.note: The music module is built from committed public datasets.

domains.label: Decision and human review
domains.headline: The repo should route decisions, not finalize them alone.
domains.note: Trust posture is C+, year support lands at 0.222, and the average confidence spread is 0.039. That is enough to guide review and prioritization, but high-stakes calls still need a human.

evidence.label: Trust score
evidence.note: Public music trust surface: coverage, source balance, and freshness.

evidence.label: Time support
evidence.note: Explicit years count more than inferred ones when the question is trend.

evidence.label: Pass rate
evidence.note: Visible quality gates that passed in the current contracted datasets.

evidence.label: Human review
evidence.note: A visible guard against auto-escalating soft evidence.

questions: Would this claim still hold if the inferred-year rows were removed?
Are we describing a fairness gap, or flattening a genre or platform confounder into one story?
Is this surface helping a human review better, or is it being asked to make the decision by itself?
What extra metadata would turn the weakest governance grade into something more defensible?

contribution_paths.title: Strengthen fairness tests
contribution_paths.detail: Add deeper subgroup checks, counterfactual analysis, or stronger uncertainty explanations where current effect sizes stay large.

contribution_paths.title: Improve public-data rights posture
contribution_paths.detail: Document what a future consent, deletion, or lawful-basis workflow would need before this surface could handle private user data.

contribution_paths.title: Expand year and source coverage carefully
contribution_paths.detail: The trust surface improves when more rows carry explicit time and language labels without pretending certainty where the source is thin.

contribution_paths.title: Keep the boundary legible in the UI
contribution_paths.detail: Refine the browser flow so readers can see when a result is strong enough to act on and when it still needs a person in the loop.
